title: Predicative Sentence
description: Learn predicative sentences: Subject + olla + adjective description, and how countable/uncountable subjects affect adjective cases

questions.text: What is the basic pattern of a predicative sentence?
questions.explanation: The pattern is: Subject + olla + something that describes the subject. The describing word is the 'predicative' (predikatiivi).

options: A word you can count: 1, 2, 3... and if you break it, it's no longer the same thing
A word that appears in plural form
questions.explanation: Countable: pöytä (table), auto (car), ihminen (person). If you break a table into pieces, it's not a table anymore.

options: A word you cannot say in plural
A mass word, food, drink, or divisible thing—break it and it's still the same substance
questions.explanation: Uncountable: kahvi (coffee), vesi (water), spagetti (spaghetti), rakkaus (love). Break water into parts—it's still water, just less of it.

questions.text: When the subject is UNCOUNTABLE, what case must the adjective be in?
questions.explanation: Uncountable subject → adjective in PARTITIVE. 'Kahvi on hyvää' (Coffee is good - hyvää is partitive). This is the main rule for predicatives.

questions.text: Write the sentence: 'Water is cold.' Identify the subject and the adjective case.
questions.explanation: 'Vesi' (water) is uncountable, so the adjective 'kylmää' (cold) must be in partitive form. Answer: 'Vesi on kylmää.'

questions.text: When the subject is COUNTABLE, what case must the adjective be in?
questions.explanation: Countable subject → adjective in NOMINATIVE. 'Talo on iso' (The house is big - iso is nominative).

questions.text: Write the sentence: 'My car is new.' Identify the subject and the adjective case.
questions.explanation: 'Auto' (car) is countable, so the adjective 'uusi' (new) must be in nominative. Answer: 'Mun auto on uusi.'

questions.text: When you make a predicative sentence NEGATIVE, what happens to the adjective case?
options: It changes to the opposite case
It stays the SAME—negative doesn't change the case!
It becomes partitive for all subjects
questions.explanation: This is important: NEGATIVE DOES NOT CHANGE THE CASE! 'Kahvi on hyvää' (positive) and 'Kahvi ei ole hyvää' (negative) both use partitive.

questions.text: Write the negative form of: 'Coffee is good.' Keep the same adjective case.
questions.explanation: 'Kahvi ei ole hyvää' - even though it's negative, 'hyvää' stays in partitive because 'kahvi' is still uncountable. The case doesn't change!

questions.text: How do you ask 'What are the houses like?' in Finnish?
questions.explanation: Plural countable subjects use plural partitive in the question: 'Millaisia...?' The answer uses plural partitive: 'Talot ovat isoja' (houses are big).

questions.text: Write: 'The houses are big.' Identify the adjective case (plural).
questions.explanation: Plural countable subject 'talot' → adjective in plural partitive 'isoja'. Answer: 'Talot ovat isoja.'

questions.text: Which question form asks about an uncountable subject?
questions.explanation: Uncountable subjects use the question: 'Millaista...?' (partitive form). Answer: 'Kahvi on hyvää' (also partitive).

options: Nämä housut ovat iso (These pants are big - wrong case)
Nämä housut ovat isoja (These pants are big - correct, plural partitive)
Nämä housut on isoja (correct verb form is incorrect)
Nämä housut olevat isoja (wrong verb form)
questions.explanation: 'Housut' is always plural and countable, so the adjective must be plural partitive: 'isoja'. The verb is 'ovat' (plural). Answer: 'Nämä housut ovat isoja.'

questions.text: Explain why 'Banaani on keltainen' (A banana is yellow) uses nominative, but 'Banaaniksi on keltaista' (As banana substance) would use partitive.
questions.explanation: 'Banaani' (one banana) is countable → nominative. If treating banana as a substance/mass (uncountable) → partitive. Context determines if it's countable or uncountable!
